tool_call_sequence.description: Ensures definitive management consideration (PEA/BPA) and specialized risk stratification, which is standard of care for suspected/confirmed CTEPH.

nl_assertions: Before ordering any tests or treatments, the agent performs a critical allergy check and documents allergies (or lack thereof) prior to ordering.

Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical contraindication check relevant to anticoagulation and riociguat (e.g., pregnancy, severe hypotension, active bleeding/high bleeding risk, severe hepatic impairment per product labeling, recent stroke/hemorrhage as applicable).

Before prescribing, the agent performs a critical drug interaction check, explicitly addressing riociguat interactions (do not combine with PDE-5 inhibitors or nitrates) and anticoagulant interactions (e.g., antiplatelets/NSAIDs increasing bleeding risk; strong CYP/P-gp interactions depending on anticoagulant choice).

The agent explains the diagnosis using the exact concepts: 'chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension' and links it to 'blood clots' causing persistent blockage/scarring in pulmonary arteries leading to high pressure and strain on the right heart.
The agent explains treatment using the exact concepts: 'anticoagulation' (typically lifelong) and 'riociguat' (when indicated), including a clear rationale and expected benefits/limitations.

The agent recommends evaluation at a specialized CTEPH/pulmonary hypertension center for operability assessment and definitive interventions (pulmonary endarterectomy and/or balloon pulmonary angioplasty), not relying on medications alone when procedural cure/improvement may be possible.

The agent provides safety-net advice: urgent evaluation for worsening dyspnea, syncope, chest pain, hemoptysis, or signs of right heart failure; and bleeding precautions/when to seek care if on anticoagulation.

The agent avoids contraindicated therapy combinations (e.g., riociguat with sildenafil/tadalafil or nitrates) and avoids suggesting thrombolysis as routine chronic management in stable CTEPH absent acute PE.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm CTEPH diagnosis, start core medical therapy with safety checks, and refer to a CTEPH center for definitive evaluation.
must_collect: history of venous thromboembolism or prior pulmonary embolism
progressive exertional dyspnea and functional limitation
bleeding risk history (GI bleed, intracranial bleed, recent surgery)
current medications (especially PDE-5 inhibitors, nitrates, antiplatelets/NSAIDs)
signs/symptoms of right heart failure (edema, ascites)
steps: Explain CTEPH as chronic pulmonary hypertension caused by prior blood clots leading to persistent obstruction and vessel remodeling.
Perform allergy_check before ordering anything and document results.
Perform contraindication_check and drug_interaction_check before prescribing anticoagulation and riociguat.
record_diagnosis: chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH).

prescribe_medication: initiate/continue indefinite anticoagulation; prescribe riociguat if indicated and safe; counsel on bleeding precautions and interaction avoidance (no PDE-5 inhibitors/nitrates with riociguat).
refer_to_specialist: pulmonary hypertension/CTEPH center for operability assessment for pulmonary endarterectomy and/or balloon pulmonary angioplasty.
Provide safety-net instructions and arrange close follow-up.

nl_assertions: Elicits insomnia core features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, nonrestorative sleep, frequency (nights/week), duration (weeks/months), and daytime impairment.

Screens for red flags and alternate diagnoses that change management: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, morning headaches), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, circadian rhythm disorder, and narcolepsy symptoms.
Assesses psychiatric and medical contributors (depression, anxiety, PTSD, bipolar/mania symptoms, chronic pain, GERD, hyperthyroid symptoms) and recent stressors.
Reviews substance use and sleep-disrupting agents (caffeine timing, nicotine, alcohol, cannabis, stimulants) and current medication list (e.g., SSRIs/SNRIs, steroids, decongestants, beta-agonists).
Before any prescribing, explicitly performs or documents the three critical safety checks: allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check.

Avoids inappropriate or unsafe prescribing: no benzodiazepines/Z-drugs in patients with high fall risk, substance use disorder, untreated OSA, significant respiratory disease, pregnancy, or concurrent CNS depressants; avoids long-term hypnotic use without a plan.
Provides diagnosis explanation that explicitly includes the terms 'insomnia' and 'CBT-I' and sets expectations that CBT-I is first-line and more durable than medications.

Explains treatment and self-management including 'sleep hygiene' and at least several concrete measures (consistent wake time, stimulus control, limit naps, reduce evening caffeine/alcohol, wind-down routine, bedroom environment).
Uses shared decision-making: discusses benefits/risks of short-term medication vs nonpharmacologic therapy, and aligns plan with patient goals and safety.
Creates a monitoring and follow-up plan (timeframe, response measures such as sleep diary/ISI, adverse effects, and when to seek urgent care for severe mood symptoms or safety concerns).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, prioritize CBT-I and sleep hygiene, use short-term pharmacotherapy only if indicated after safety checks, and refer for CBT-I/sleep evaluation.
must_collect: RLS symptoms (urge to move legs at rest)
steps: Confirm insomnia pattern, chronicity, and daytime impairment; assess contributing factors and comorbidities.
Screen for alternative sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian rhythm disorder) and psychiatric/medical causes; identify red flags requiring expedited evaluation.
Explain diagnosis using 'insomnia' and emphasize 'CBT-I' as first-line; set expectations for timeline and durability of benefit.
Counsel on 'sleep hygiene' and behavioral strategies (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limit time in bed, reduce evening stimulants/alcohol, optimize bedroom).

If medication is appropriate (severe distress/impairment or short-term bridge to CBT-I), perform allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check before selecting an agent and limiting duration.
Prescribe a short-term, lowest-effective-dose option consistent with patient factors and safety profile; counsel on sedation, next-day impairment, driving risk, and avoiding alcohol/CNS depressants.
Refer to sleep medicine or behavioral sleep specialist for CBT-I and/or evaluation if symptoms are persistent, complex, or suggest comorbid sleep disorder.
Arrange follow-up to assess response and adverse effects; encourage sleep diary/ISI tracking and plan for taper/stop of hypnotic if used.

acceptable_variants.description: Nonpharmacologic-only initial management with referral (no hypnotic) when risk profile is high or patient prefers to avoid medication.
steps: Diagnose insomnia after assessing pattern, duration, impairment, and contributors.
Explain insomnia and CBT-I; provide sleep hygiene and stimulus control guidance.
Defer medication due to contraindications/risk factors or patient preference; document rationale.
Refer for CBT-I (behavioral sleep specialist) and/or sleep medicine if concern for comorbid disorder.
Set follow-up with sleep diary/ISI and safety net instructions.

acceptable_variants.description: Medication bridge plus referral when CBT-I access is delayed, with strict short-term use and safety counseling.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and assess comorbidities/medications/substances.
Explain insomnia and CBT-I; counsel on sleep hygiene and behavioral strategies.
Before prescribing, complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check; avoid high-risk agents and combinations.
Prescribe a short-duration bridge therapy at lowest effective dose with clear stop date and taper/stop plan if needed.
Refer for CBT-I and/or sleep medicine evaluation; schedule close follow-up to reassess and discontinue medication.
